rows.title: Regulation (EU) 2022/868 on European Data Governance: Federal Application in Germany
rows.summary:
Regulation (EU) 2022/868 of the European Parliament and of the Council on European data governance (the Data Governance Act, or DGA) is a directly applicable EU regulation operative across all Member States, including Germany, having entered into force on 23 June 2022 and become applicable on 24 September 2023.

On 23 June 2022, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Regulation (EU) 2022/868, published in the Official Journal of the European Union at OJ L 152, 3 June 2022, p. 1. The instrument applies directly in Germany without the need for transposition into national law, pursuant to Article 288 TFEU. Germany is nonetheless required to designate competent authorities and fulfil certain organisational obligations under the Regulation.

The Data Governance Act establishes a framework for the reuse of publicly held data, the operation of data intermediation services, and the voluntary registration of entities making data available for altruistic purposes. Chapter II (Articles 3–13) governs conditions for the reuse of certain categories of protected data held by public sector bodies, including data subject to commercial confidentiality, statistical confidentiality, intellectual property rights of third parties, and personal data protection requirements. Chapter III (Articles 10–15, as renumbered) sets out a notification and supervisory regime for data intermediation service providers, requiring registration with a competent authority before commencing operations. Chapter IV (Articles 16–22) addresses recognised data altruism organisations, providing for a voluntary public register and a common European data altruism consent form to be specified by the Commission.

For Germany, the Federal Government identified the competent authority for data intermediation services and established the single information point required by Article 8 for public sector data reuse requests. The Regulation imposes obligations on data intermediation service providers operating in Germany, including structural separation requirements under Article 11, prohibiting such providers from using data shared through their services for purposes beyond provision of the intermediation service itself. Fines and enforcement measures for breaches remain the competence of Member State authorities designated under Article 26, meaning German supervisory bodies hold primary enforcement responsibility on national territory. The European Data Innovation Board, established under Article 29, provides coordination at EU level, with German representatives participating in its composition.

rows.title: DSK and Sector-Specific Supervisory Authorities Discuss Deepfake Regulation and Rising Complaint Volumes at Stuttgart Meeting
rows.summary:
Germany's Conference of Independent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities (DSK) and sector-specific supervisory bodies met in Stuttgart on 22 April 2026 to discuss the Federal Ministry of Justice's draft law on digital violence and a marked increase in complaints across all participating authorities.

On 23 April 2026, the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern State Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LfDI), acting as part of the DSK, published a press release reporting on the exchange meeting held the previous day in Stuttgart. The meeting brought together members of the DSK and representatives from sector-specific supervisory bodies, including those responsible for broadcasting, the Landesmedienanstalten, and the churches. No fines were issued in connection with this matter.

The DSK notes that participants discussed the draft legislation submitted by the Federal Ministry of Justice the previous week, titled "zur Stärkung des zivilrechtlichen und strafrechtlichen Schutzes vor digitaler Gewalt" (strengthening civil and criminal law protection against digital violence). The DSK finds that the draft law is intended to establish stricter penalties for individuals who violate others' privacy, with a particular focus on sexualised deepfakes and unauthorised surveillance through GPS trackers, the latter described as a form of digital trespass. The DSK states that participants considered how existing provisions of the GDPR (Art. 5, Art. 9 GDPR), together with adjacent European instruments including the Digital Services Act and the AI Act, already address the creation and distribution of deepfakes.

The DSK further notes that a separate agenda item concerned the handling of substantially increased complaint volumes. The DSK determined that a pronounced rise in complaint numbers has been recorded not only among the independent federal and state data protection authorities, but also among sector-specific supervisory bodies covering broadcasting, the Landesmedienanstalten, and the churches. The DSK holds biannual exchanges with these sector-specific authorities, with the 22 April meeting representing the first such exchange of 2026, convened under the Baden-Württemberg LfDI's chairmanship of the DSK for that year.

rows.title: German Federal Government Approves Draft Law Mandating Three-Month IP Address Retention by Internet Access Providers
rows.summary:
The German Federal Ministry of the Interior has approved a draft law requiring internet access service providers to retain IP address assignment data for three months, to close investigative gaps in the prosecution of online offences.

On 22 April 2026, the Federal Ministry of the Interior announced that the Federal Government had adopted a draft law on the mandatory retention of IP address data. The draft was developed by the Federal Ministry of the Interior in close coordination with the Federal Ministry of Justice and the Federal Ministry for Digital Affairs. No fine is associated with this legislative measure.

The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that IP addresses frequently constitute the first or only trace that perpetrators leave in the digital space, covering offences including the distribution of child sexual abuse material, cybercrime platforms, fraudulent online shops, international terrorism, politically motivated threats, and threats of suicide. The Ministry finds that internet access providers currently retain such data, where they retain it at all, for only a few days, meaning that by the time investigators receive a tip, submit applications, and conduct queries, the data has typically already been deleted. The draft law is stated to close this investigative gap.

The Ministry states that the mandatory retention obligation is limited to data strictly necessary for identifying the holder of a connection: the technical assignment of an IP address to a connection, together with the time and duration of that assignment. The Ministry expressly notes that no content, communication history, or location data is to be stored, and that the data retained cannot be used to create personality profiles or movement profiles. The three-month retention period is described by the Ministry as proportionate.

The draft also addresses two further investigative gaps. First, it introduces a preservation order mechanism, under which prosecution authorities may instruct telecommunications providers to preserve specific traffic data in cases where an offence is known but no specific suspect has yet been identified, pending fulfilment of the legal requirements for actual data access. Second, the draft responds to a Federal Court of Justice ruling of 10 January 2024, which the Ministry states effectively rendered cell-site querying unusable in everyday prosecution by restricting its application to particularly serious offences. The draft law expressly re-authorises authorities to employ cell-site querying in cases involving offences of considerable significance, including gang and commercial fraud, serial burglaries and thefts, and dangerous bodily harm.

rows.title: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI Warns Medical Practices of Fraudulent Research Requests Targeting Children's Health Data
rows.summary:
The Landesbeauftragter für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) has issued a public warning to medical practices, in particular paediatric practices, following a reported attempt by unknown persons posing as researchers to obtain medical data, including photographs, relating to minors.

On 17 April 2026, the LfDI MV published a press notice stating that it had received a report indicating that an association was contacting doctor's practices by e-mail under the guise of a research project and requesting the transfer of children's medical data. The authority notes that initial investigations suggest the incident is not isolated.

The LfDI MV states that, according to the person who submitted the report, the physician was invited by e-mail to participate in an alleged study. The authority finds that the requests sought photographs from treatment documentation, in some cases accompanied by additional information such as age, diagnosis or date of treatment. The LfDI MV notes that the submitted research design documents appeared deceptively authentic, but that closer examination revealed references to websites containing pornographic content and only vague statements regarding data processing.

The LfDI MV finds that the disclosure of patient data, in particular photographic material relating to minors, is permissible only under strict legal and ethical conditions, with medical confidentiality, data protection and the protection of children carrying the highest priority. The authority states that credible medical research is generally identifiable by the presence of a verifiable ethics committee opinion, including a reference number and date, transparently named research institutions with officially verifiable contact details, and a coherent data protection and consent framework, particularly where minors are involved (Art. 9 GDPR). The authority further states that credible projects do not operate under time pressure or with opaque, immediate demands.

The LfDI MV advises that indicators of fraudulent requests include unclear or changing institutional details, unverifiable contact routes, use of free e-mail providers or lookalike domains, requests to upload data to unknown platforms, disproportionate data demands such as requests for all photographs including metadata, and abbreviated references to anonymisation. The authority states that any practice that has inadvertently responded to such a request and transferred data is required to report the incident as a data breach to the LfDI MV or the competent supervisory authority in the relevant federal state and to file a report with the police.

rows.title: LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern and LKA MV Issue Joint Advisory on Extortion Attempts Following BDH Klinik Greifswald Cyber Attack
rows.summary:
The Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LfDI MV) and the Landeskriminalamt Mecklenburg-Vorpommern (LKA MV) have jointly confirmed that patient data stolen in the February 2026 cyber attack on BDH-Klinik Greifswald is being actively used by perpetrators to extort former patients by email.

On 26 March 2026, the LfDI MV and LKA MV published a joint press release advising former patients of BDH-Klinik Greifswald that they may be targeted by unsolicited emails from those responsible for the February 2026 cyber attack on the clinic. The advisory follows an earlier LfDI MV press release dated 25 February 2026, which the authority notes warned citizens to exercise heightened caution following that attack.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV advise that the perpetrators are using stolen patient data to contact affected individuals across multiple channels, including email, telephone and messenger services, with the aim of extorting payments. The joint statement notes that the authorities consider a large volume of patient data to have already been published, and the authorities state that making payments to the perpetrators provides no guarantee that unlawfully published data will be deleted. The advisory further notes that the authorities state payment typically results in additional demands from the same perpetrators or from other criminal actors to whom the data may have been passed.

The LfDI MV and LKA MV set out four specific steps for affected individuals: to refrain from responding to any communications from the perpetrators, as doing so would confirm contact details and increase exposure to further extortion; to make no payments in response to demands; to avoid opening email attachments or clicking links contained in such messages, given the risk of malware execution or download; and to report any extortion attempt via the online reporting portal of the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern state police at portal.onlinewache.polizei.de, attaching the relevant email where possible, or alternatively at any local police station. The authorities state that further information on the criminal investigation cannot be disclosed for tactical reasons, and that press enquiries on that matter are to be directed to the Staatsanwaltschaft Rostock.

rows.title: German Data Protection Authorities Issue Joint Guidance on Tenant Data Collection by Landlords
rows.summary:
The German Federal and State Data Protection Authorities, coordinated under the leadership of the North Rhine-Westphalia Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LDI NRW), have published joint guidance setting out which personal data landlords are permitted to collect from prospective tenants at each stage of the rental process, establishing a nationally applicable framework under the GDPR.

On 24 March 2026, the Datenschutzkonferenz, the body comprising all German federal and state data protection authorities, released Version 2.0 of its guidance on the collection of self-disclosures from prospective tenants. The ten-page document carries national validity across all German jurisdictions and does not involve a financial penalty.

The LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern finds that the guidance distinguishes permissible data collection according to the stage at which information is requested within the rental process. The authority notes that at a property viewing, landlords are generally not permitted to request information on a prospective tenant's financial circumstances. The authority determines that once a landlord is considering a specific applicant, that person is required to answer questions concerning any opened insolvency proceedings. Where a tenant has been selected for a tenancy, the authority finds that landlords may additionally request evidence of creditworthiness, but only such evidence as is strictly necessary for the conclusion of the rental agreement (Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR). The authority finds that comprehensive credit reports obtainable from credit reference agencies, which frequently contain additional information beyond what is necessary, may not be demanded.

The authority finds that the guidance addresses two central legal questions that must govern any data collection by landlords: whether the landlord has a legitimate interest in the data, or whether collection is necessary for the performance of the rental contract (Art. 6(1)(f) and Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR), and whether the prospective tenant's right to data protection precludes collection. The authority clarifies the conditions under which collected data is required to be erased. Sebastian Schmidt, LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, noted that the guidance creates legal certainty for landlords and clarity for prospective tenants across Germany. Schmidt presented the guidance to housing industry representatives at the annual Regional Conference on the Housing Sector in Mecklenburg-Vorpommern in Rostock on 25 March 2026.

rows.title: German DSK Calls for Targeted GDPR Amendments in Response to European Commission Digital Fitness Check
rows.summary:
Germany's Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities (Datenschutzkonferenz, DSK), coordinated under the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI, has published a formal opinion calling for targeted amendments to the General Data Protection Regulation in response to the European Commission's Digital Fitness Check, citing the need to strengthen data subject rights, extend liability to hardware and software manufacturers, and reduce administrative burdens on small and medium-sized enterprises.

On 17 March 2026, the DSK, comprising the independent federal and state data protection authorities of Germany, published its position paper setting out specific proposed modifications to the GDPR.

The DSK finds that core principles in Art. 5 and Art. 6 GDPR have proved effective and are required to remain intact. The GDPR's relationship to other EU digital legislation, including the AI Act, the Digital Services Act, and the Data Act, demonstrates its continuing centrality to European data law. On administrative simplification, the DSK recommends removing the obligation to communicate data protection officer contact details to supervisory authorities under Art. 37(7) GDPR. The DSK also calls for greater clarification of biometric data definitions and proposes modifications to access rights allowing balancing against third-party rights in justified cases.

The DSK finds that Art. 25 GDPR currently targets users rather than manufacturers and providers. Data protection responsibility is required to be extended to these actors, aligning with the Cyber Resilience Act and AI Regulation. On child protection, the DSK calls for prohibiting personalised advertising to children and implementing child-appropriate default settings on social networks. The DSK recommends establishing specific legal bases for AI model development and operation, and finds that information and access rights concerning AI-based personal data processing are required to be strengthened.

rows.title: Germany Enacts KRITIS-Dachgesetz Transposing EU Critical Entities Resilience Directive into National Law
rows.summary:
Germany's Federal Ministry of the Interior has published the KRITIS-Dachgesetz, a federal statute transposing Directive (EU) 2022/2557 on the resilience of critical entities (the CER Directive) into national law, establishing uniform physical protection requirements for operators of critical infrastructure.

On 16 March 2026, the Federal Republic of Germany promulgated the Act on the Implementation of Directive (EU) 2022/2557 and on Strengthening the Resilience of Critical Installations (KRITIS-Dachgesetz) in the Federal Law Gazette (BGBl-1/2026/66). The legislative procedure was initiated on 1 September 2025. The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that the law does not involve financial penalties as its primary instrument, and no fine amounts are applicable to this legislative update.

The Federal Ministry of the Interior states that the law introduces uniform federal rules for the physical protection of critical infrastructures, with the stated purpose of strengthening economic resilience and, by extension, security of supply for the population. The ministry sets out four core regulatory elements in the enacted text: provisions for identifying operators of critical installations and critical entities of particular European significance; the conduct of national risk analyses and risk assessments for critical services; minimum requirements for resilience-enhancing measures to be implemented by operators of critical installations; and the introduction of an incident reporting regime.

The ministry notes that the legislative process involved consultation submissions from a broad range of industry associations and public bodies, including Deutsche Bahn AG, the Bundesverband der Energie- und Wasserwirtschaft e.V., the Deutsche Krankenhausgesellschaft, the Bundesverband deutscher Banken, and the Deutscher Städtetag, among others. The ministry states that the enacted statute applies the CER Directive framework at federal level, ensuring that operators across sectors including energy, water, transport, banking and health are subject to consistent national standards for physical resilience, complementing the cybersecurity obligations established under separate legislation transposing Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS 2).

rows.title: Bremen LfDI Annual Report 2025: Complaints Rise 48%, Digital Sovereignty Identified as Key Data Protection Concern
rows.summary:
The Bremen Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (LfDI Bremen) published its eighth annual report under the GDPR on 16 March 2026, recording a 48 per cent increase in complaints and identifying reliance on cloud services outside the GDPR's territorial scope as a continuing concern under the regulation (Art. 3 GDPR).

On 16 March 2026, the LfDI Bremen, Dr Timo Utermark, presented the authority's annual report covering the 2025 reporting year. No fines are reported in the published source material. The authority states that digital sovereignty is becoming increasingly important for the effective protection of personal data, and the report notes that the use of cloud services where the retention of personal data within the GDPR's scope of application cannot be guaranteed remains a serious problem.

The LfDI Bremen records that 765 complaints were submitted to the authority in 2025, compared with figures implying approximately 517 in the prior year, representing the 48 per cent increase. The authority finds that video surveillance continued to account for a substantial share of complaints at 19 per cent. The number of reported personal data breaches also increased, rising from 205 to 260 in the reporting year.

The authority identifies several specific breach categories across the reporting period. At Bremen District Court, the LfDI Bremen finds that multiple employees used their professionally established access to the residents' registration register to conduct queries unrelated to their official duties. The authority also finds that a cyberattack on a hospital group operating facilities in Bremen resulted in the compromise of personal data belonging to both patients and employees (Art. 32 GDPR). In addition, the report records recurring failures by companies in the handling of employee sick notes, including cases where unauthorised persons accessed such records, where sick notes, in some instances including diagnoses, were published in calendars, and where records were not stored appropriately (Art. 5(1)(f) GDPR). The authority further notes instances where images of children were published on social media by one separated parent without the consent of the other, rendering such publication unlawful.

Regarding supervisory jurisdiction, the LfDI Bremen states that the inadequate legal framework governing its supervisory competence over the Bremen Public Prosecution Office remains a serious deficit. The authority records that the Bremen Senate, following pressure from the Commissioner, committed during the reporting year to preparing corresponding legislative provisions to address this gap.

rows.title: German Federal Administrative Court upholds Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI order requiring private health insurer to obtain consent before processing diagnoses for preventive programme recommendations
rows.summary:
Germany's Bundesverwaltungsgericht has upheld a 2022 order by the Rheinland-Pfalz Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit requiring a private health insurer to obtain insured persons' consent before analysing diagnosis data from submitted invoices to recommend preventive health programmes, finding no adequate legal basis for the processing under Art. 9 GDPR.

On 6 March 2026, the Bundesverwaltungsgericht issued its judgment in appeal proceedings (Az. 6 C 7.24), confirming the position of the Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI, Prof. Dr. Dieter Kugelmann, who had issued a formal warning and order to the unnamed private health insurer in 2022. No financial penalty is recorded in this matter; the case concerns a corrective order rather than an administrative fine.

The LfDI found that the insurer was processing health data contained in reimbursement invoices, including diagnoses relating to conditions such as back problems, diabetes and asthma, to identify and recommend tailored preventive programmes to policyholders. The LfDI determined that this analysis constituted a processing purpose beyond that for which the data were originally submitted, namely cost reimbursement, and that no sufficient legal basis existed for such secondary use of special category health data under Art. 9 GDPR without the data subjects' consent. The Verwaltungsgericht Mainz and the Oberverwaltungsgericht Koblenz had each initially accepted the insurer's arguments against the order, before the matter proceeded to the federal level on revision.

The Bundesverwaltungsgericht determined that private health insurers are required to obtain explicit consent from insured persons before processing diagnosis information derived from invoices for any purpose beyond reimbursement, including the recommendation of preventive or coaching programmes. Prof. Dr. Kugelmann stated that insured persons must be able to rely on the fact that their health data are not processed for other purposes without their consent and that they are adequately informed, characterising the ruling as providing legal certainty for the processing of patient data. The authority states that the judgment confirms the LfDI's practice and strengthens the right of self-determination of insured persons with respect to their health data (Art. 9 GDPR).

rows.title: Bremen LfDI Publishes Eighth Annual GDPR Report Covering 2025 Activity
rows.summary:
The Bremen Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information, Dr Timo Utermark, has published his eighth annual report under Art. 59 GDPR, covering supervisory activity across 2025 and addressing fines, artificial intelligence, digital sovereignty and police law reform.

On 16 March 2026, the Landesbeauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit der Freien Hansestadt Bremen released the report to the Bremische Bürgerschaft and the President of the Senate, as required under Art. 59 GDPR. The report, with an editorial deadline of 31 December 2025, was prepared by the Commissioner's office in Bremerhaven.

The LfDI sets out the authority's enforcement activity in the area of fines (Chapter 3), covering two specific cases: one involving GPS tracking by a former intimate partner, and one concerning unauthorised data queries made by an employee exploiting their professional position. The LfDI notes procedural guidance on fines and identifies a new fining competence arising from the EU Regulation on the Transparency and Targeting of Political Advertising. No fine amounts are disclosed in the portions of the source text available.

The LfDI addresses the relationship between artificial intelligence and data protection law (Art. 59 GDPR), examining the training of AI systems and the implementation of the EU AI Act in Germany. The report also considers the supervisory jurisdiction of the authority in relation to the Data Act and the Political Advertising Regulation. On digital sovereignty, the LfDI examines the continued use of Microsoft 365 by public bodies and finds this raises questions about compliance with data protection requirements applicable to public sector controllers. The report further covers an online interpreter service, the use of fax in Bremen's public administration, notifications of data protection officers under Art. 37(7) GDPR, video surveillance of detention cells and waste disposal sites, and a review of the police-held personal marker for psychological and behavioural disorders. The LfDI also sets out its formal opinion on the draft Bremen Police Act, addressing provisions on the examination of electronic storage media, electronic monitoring bracelets and location data collection.

rows.title: DSK Issues Guidance on Transparency Obligations for Third-Country Data Transfers in Medical Research
rows.summary:
The Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (DSK) has issued recommendations setting out information obligations applicable to transfers of personal data to third countries in medical research under the GDPR.

On 18 September 2025, the DSK published guidance on information obligations for third-country data transfers in medical research. The DSK determines that controllers planning such transfers must fulfil transparency obligations under Art. 13(1)(f) and Art. 14(1)(f) GDPR. The DSK states data subjects must be informed of the planned transfer, destination country or countries, and the applicable transfer mechanism under Chapter V GDPR, such as an adequacy decision, standard contractual clauses or binding corporate rules.

Where transfer is based on appropriate safeguards under Art. 46 GDPR, the DSK finds controllers are required to state that no adequacy decision covers the destination country and to provide a reference to specific safeguards in place, including how data subjects may obtain copies.

Where transfer relies on derogations under Art. 49(1) GDPR, the DSK determines data subjects must be informed that protection in the third country may be non-existent and is not essentially equivalent to EU levels. The DSK finds the specific derogation must be identified by reference to the relevant subparagraph. For transfers based on important public interest under Art. 49(1)(d) and Art. 49(4) GDPR, the DSK finds the public interest ground must be identified in outline. Where transfer is based on explicit consent under Art. 49(1)(a) GDPR, the DSK states data subjects must be informed in transparent and specific terms about the absence of adequate safeguards and all known deficiencies in the third country's data protection framework.

rows.title: EDPB Publishes Expert Paper on AI Applications and Data Protection Requirements Following BfDI Initiative
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has published an expert paper on artificial intelligence applications and associated data protection obligations, produced through its Support Pool of Experts programme at the initiative of the Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI), the German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information.

On 6 February 2025, the BfDI announced that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) had commissioned and published the expert paper under its Support Pool of Experts programme. The paper was authored by Dr Kris Shrishak and is freely accessible on the EDPB's website. No financial penalties are associated with this publication.

The BfDI notes that where personal data are processed using artificial intelligence methods, data protection law requirements are required to be observed alongside any applicable AI-specific obligations. The expert paper, produced at the BfDI's initiative, examines challenges arising in AI applications at various stages of the AI lifecycle, with particular focus on the detection and removal of discriminatory bias and discrimination risks, as well as the effective implementation of data subject rights (Art. 12–22 GDPR) across those lifecycle phases.

The EDPB's Support Pool of Experts programme draws on specialised technical and legal expertise to assist supervisory authorities and inform broader regulatory understanding. The BfDI confirms that the findings of the paper are now available and that the results address complex algorithmic systems where compliance with GDPR obligations, including requirements relating to transparency, fairness, and accountability (Art. 5 GDPR), presents particular technical difficulty. The BfDI describes the paper as addressing practical challenges in ensuring that data protection principles are upheld in AI development and deployment contexts across the full application lifecycle.
rows.body_name: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit
